item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for clearing that up. Yes, Dr. Lang is quoting Oppenheimer here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It should be "I am time, destroyer of all the worlds" but Oppenheimer chose to word it differently than most standard translations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Robert_Oppenheimer

That could be an Oppenheimer reference, who actually said it as "I am become Death".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds,"

Wouldn't it be "I have become Death"? Or is there a secret grammar loophole in play here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After thinking about for a second, you are absolutely correct. I really don't know how to portray what it is that I'm trying to portray, but this absolutely isn't the way to do it. I enjoy reading and I've certainly never seen any of my favorite authors resort to a stream of question marks and exclamation marks.

But that's what this site is for. Any suggestions?

EDIT: It was such a glaring embarrassment that I just decided to change it right away. I don't think I broke the flow, but hopefully it's less of an amateurish eyesore now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's my biggest problem with this tale, and one that has me wavering between downvote and novote. The section starting here:

Dead.
Gone.

to:

Why??!!!???????????????????????
ANSWER ME?!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

reads profoundly absurd and overly dramatic. People think like this from time to time. I've done it. But it doesn't read well whatsoever. It rarely, if ever, reads well to be so blunt and direct to that degree. I almost stopped reading because it was just over the top. And while you're trying to go for a realistic thought process, it just reads in a really not-good manner at times, but it's especially apparent in the quoted section. Either tone it down or cut it down dramatically.

This tale otherwise goes in a unique direction, and I appreciate that and the angle you're going for, as well as how you're trying to portray Dr. Lang and his situation. I don't think the idea sounds particularly compelling but there are moments where you rescue it and pull it off. The sudden ending really got me the moment I finished, and the final section was good overall. I just don't know if I can neutral-vote like this.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: When I started reading this, I hadn't even considered that this might end depressingly. I was excited, looking forward to learned about Xyank and to see how they either fix 2100 or fuck everything up even worse — and then it ended. Abruptly, unsatisfyingly. At first I was left confused, thinking "…is….is that it?", and naturally I came to the discussion tab — and after reading your reasoning, I think this is perfect. Of course there are going to be people trying to breach security in the Foundation, and of course most of them are going to fail spectacularly mundanely. As you said, too many stories have guns and guards be simple obstacles to overcome, so the hero can be heroic. I feel saddened by Dr. Lang's tragic end — but it feels real, and I'm happy I feel sad (if that makes any sense). Well done, +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, okay, I get it now,

+ show block
- hide block

I think the issue was on my end in a way. I came into the story expecting a story about… well, something anomalous (Especially given that both objects involved are temporal ones) rather than a story about a man failing. So I completely focused my attention in the story on the wrong things and when the end showed up I looked back up trying to figure out what I'd missed instead of being depressed.

I honestly half expected him to fail and for it all to end in fire - but it's odd, it's as if because this takes place in the foundation universe, I just didn't expect him to fail in a completely mundane and human way.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
You are correct.

+ Show commentary
- Hide

EDIT: I wouldn't say, however, that I meant for this to be depressing, just that I wanted it to describe what someone going through major, life altering depression might feel, combined with the thrill of hope that there's a solution to all his problems.

The ending was something I knew I wanted in there from before I ever wrote the first word of the story. I knew I wanted the protagonist to die anti-climatically as he tries to be a hero. Too many stories, well everywhere, have guns and guards as some minor obstacle to be easily beaten. The action hero dodges a few bullets, pulls some karate or quick thinking out his ass and bypasses them with little to no resistance. I wanted this to be different, a little bit more real. He dies as he gets shot in the head trying to interrupt a temporal experiment.

And the story is told from the third person limited perspective. It's all about him, Dr. Lang. So when he dies, the story ends, because it ends for him. Does Xyank ask to speak with Schrader and end up fixing everything? Does he go back to his native timeline never to be heard again? Dr. Lang doesn't know, so we don't know. Life just ends, and when it ends like this, there's no resolution.

How many people die in car accidents on their way to work, or at work, or of heart attacks or sudden onset of illness, dying in the middle of their own personal story? Especially in the Foundation!! They don't get to wrap up their life nicely and finish everything they wanted to do. They don't even get to prepare for death. The Foundation itself must be full if instant, unprepared deaths, I wanted to explore THOSE, not the action hero who solves his problem against all odds.

I wanted to explore what would drive a person to risk their life and start doing something stupid yet "heroic" in a world full of horrors and unfathomable power, and what the likely outcome of that is.

item.title: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

2100 ends with the Foundation fucking up and ending an untold number of lives. This ends with someone who tried to fix it paying with their life. The whole point is to be depressing.

But then he's shot and it all just… ends.

Yep. He's a failure. His best efforts weren't good enough. He wasn't good enough.

The author can correct me if I'm wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I… missing something? I mean, I understand everything that's going on, Dr. Lang and his guilt and his attempt at getting help from someone versed in temporal… stuff. But then he's shot and it all just… ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In honor of SCP-2100 reaching a +100 rating, I've decided to finally post my tale about it. This is actually my favorite thing I've written for the site. I hope others find it appealing.
